analogy: Think of "Cell Cycle and Cell Division" through the chapter's own opening images: 120 BIOLOGY Are you aware that all organisms, even the largest, start their life from a single cell? You may wonder how a single cell then goes on to form such large organisms. Growth and reproduction are characteristics of cells, indeed of all living organisms.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 120 BIOLOGY, 10.1 CELL CYCLE, CELL CYCLE AND CELL DIVISION, CHAPTER 10. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Cell Cycle and Cell Division is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 120 BIOLOGY Are you aware that all organisms, even the largest, start their life from a single cell?

The text explains that you may wonder how a single cell then goes on to form such large organisms.

The text explains that growth and reproduction are characteristics of cells, indeed of all living organisms.

The text explains that all cells reproduce by dividing into two, with each parental cell giving rise to two daughter cells each time they divide.

The text explains that these newly formed daughter cells can themselves grow and divide, giving rise to a new cell population that is formed by the growth and division of a single parental cell and its progeny.

The text explains that in other words, such cycles of growth and division allow a single cell to form a structure consisting of millions of cells.

The text explains that 10.1 CELL CYCLE Cell division is a very important process in all living organisms.

The text explains that during the division of a cell, DNA replication and cell growth also take place.

The text explains that all these processes, i.e., cell division, DNA replication, and cell growth, hence, have to take place in a coordinated way to ensure correct division and formation of progeny cells containing intact genomes.

The text explains that the sequence of events by which a cell duplicates its genome, synthesises the other constituents of the cell and eventually divides into two daughter cells is termed cell cycle.

The text explains that although cell growth (in terms of cytoplasmic increase) is a continuous process, DNA synthesis occurs only during one specific stage in the cell cycle.

The text explains that the replicated chromosomes (DNA) are then distributed to daughter nuclei by a complex series of events during cell division.

The text explains that these events are themselves under genetic control.

The text explains that cELL CYCLE AND CELL DIVISION CHAPTER 10 10.1 Cell Cycle 10.2 M Phase 10.3 Significance of Mitosis 10.4 Meiosis 10.5 Significance of Meiosis

The text explains that cELL CYCLE AND CELL DIVISION 121 10.1.1 Phases of Cell Cycle A typical eukaryotic cell cycle is illustrated by human cells in culture.

The text explains that these cells divide once in approximately every 24 hours (Figure 10.1).

The text explains that from your recollection of examples of alternation of generations in plants (Chapter 3) identify plant species and stages at which mitosis is seen in haploid cells.

The text explains that though for convenience mitosis has been divided into four stages of nuclear division (karyokinesis), it is very essential to understand that cell division is a progressive process and very clear-cut lines cannot be drawn between various stages.

The text explains that the chromosomal material becomes untangled during the process of chromatin condensation (Figure 10.2 a).

The text explains that small disc-shaped structures at the surface of the centromeres are called kinetochores.

The text explains that these structures serve as the sites of attachment of spindle fibres to the chromosomes that are moved into position at the centre of the cell.

The text explains that the key features of metaphase are: lllll Spindle fibres attach to kinetochores of chromosomes. lllll Chromosomes are moved to spindle equator and get aligned along metaphase plate through spindle fibres to both poles.

The text explains that as each chromosome moves away from the equatorial plate, the centromere of each chromosome remains directed towards the pole and hence at the leading edge, with the arms of the chromosome trailing behind (Figure 10.2 c).

The text explains that the key features of meiosis are as follows: lllll Meiosis involves two sequential cycles of nuclear and cell division called meiosis I and meiosis II but only a single cycle of DNA replication. lllll Meiosis I is initiated after the parental chromosomes have replicated to produce identical sister chromatids at the S phase. lllll Meiosis involves pairing of homologous chromosomes and recombination between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes. lllll Four haploid cells are formed at the end of meiosis II.

The text explains that during this stage, chromosomes start pairing together and this process of association is called synapsis.

The text explains that electron micrographs of this stage, indicate that chromosome synapsis is accompanied by the formation of complex structure called synaptonemal complex. synaptonemal complex. synaptonemal complex. synaptonemal complex. synaptonemal complex.

The text explains that crossing over is also an enzyme-mediated process and the enzyme involved is called recombinase.

The text explains that these X-shaped structures, are called chiasmata. chiasmata. chiasmata. chiasmata. chiasmata.

The text explains that 10.5 SIGNIFICANCE OF MEIOSIS Meiosis is the mechanism by which conservation of specific chromosome number of each species is achieved across generations in sexually reproducing organisms, even though the process, per se, paradoxically, results in reduction of chromosome number by half.

The text explains that variations are very important for the process of evolution.

The text explains that the process by which this occurs is called cell division.

The text explains that (iv) Crossing over between homologous chromosomes takes place.

The text explains that describe the following: (a) synapsis (b) bivalent (c) chiasmata Draw a diagram to illustrate your answer.

The text explains that how does cytokinesis in plant cells differ from that in animal cells?

The text explains that find examples where the four daughter cells from meiosis are equal in size and where they are found unequal in size.

The text explains that distinguish anaphase of mitosis from anaphase I of meiosis.

The text explains that list the main differences between mitosis and meiosis.

The text explains that discuss with your teacher about (i) haploid insects and lower plants where cell-division occurs, and (ii) some haploid cells in higher plants where cell-division does not occur.

The text explains that can there be mitosis without DNA replication in ‘S’ phase?

The text explains that can there be DNA replication without cell division?

The text explains that analyse the events during every stage of cell cycle and notice how the following two parameters change (i) number of chromosomes (N) per cell (ii) amount of DNA content (C) per cell

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10: Cell Cycle and Cell Division

prelims_traps: Do not treat 120 BIOLOGY and 10.1 CELL CYCLE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Cell Cycle and Cell Division; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
